query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis:
Venice is highly vulnerable to relative sea-level rise (RSLR), a combination of global sea-level rise and local land subsidence. Historical records indicate sea levels rose approximately 26 cm over the past century, with current rates estimated at 4.9 mm/year [Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41612-023-00513-0), [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/338061469_The_impact_of_operating_the_mobile_barriers_in_Venice_MOSE_under_climate_change). Projections for 2100 under IPCC scenarios range from a 20-40 cm increase (RCP 4.5) to up to 80 cm (RCP 8.5) [Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41612-023-00513-0). By 2150, RSLR could reach 153 cm under high-emission scenarios (SSP5-8.5), potentially leading to temporary water levels of 3.47 m during extreme events [Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon | PreventionWeb](https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/multi-temporal-relative-sea-level-rise-scenarios-2150-venice-lagoon), [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract).

Flood frequency is expected to increase dramatically. Tides exceeding the 110 cm safeguarding threshold, which historically occurred roughly 4 times per year, are projected to occur between 20 and 250 times per year by 2100 [The projected MOSE barriers against flooding in Venice (Italy) and the expected global sea-level rise](https://www.academia.edu/10956443/The_projected_MOSE_barriers_against_flooding_in_Venice_Italy_and_the_expected_global_sea_level_rise). Without intervention, extreme 1-in-100 year events are projected to increase in magnitude by 160% by the end of the century [The projected MOSE barriers against flooding in Venice (Italy) and the expected global sea-level rise](https://www.academia.edu/10956443/The_projected_MOSE_barriers_against_flooding_in_Venice_Italy_and_the_expected_global_sea_level_rise).

The MoSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barrier system, designed to protect against surges up to 3 meters, faces a viability "tipping point" due to these projections [Boon and burden: economic performance and future perspectives of the Venice flood protection system](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10113-024-02193-9). Key operational thresholds include:
- **+0.40 m RSLR:** A projected failure threshold where wind setup (water accumulation inside the lagoon) prevents the barriers from protecting all urban settlements [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831).
- **+0.50 m SLR:** Barriers would require closing once per day on average [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/338061469_The_impact_of_operating_the_mobile_barriers_in_Venice_MOSE_under_climate_change).
- **+0.75 m SLR:** The lagoon would be closed more often than it is open [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/338061469_The_impact_of_operating_the_mobile_barriers_in_Venice_MOSE_under_climate_change).
- **RCP 8.5 (2100):** The system may need to remain closed for approximately 260 days per year [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/338061469_The_impact_of_operating_the_mobile_barriers_in_Venice_MOSE_under_climate_change).

Frequent closures pose severe secondary risks, including ecological degradation (hypoxia and a tripling of water renewal times), restricted sediment flux for salt marshes, and economic losses to the Port of Venice estimated up to 1.3 million EUR annually [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/338061469_The_impact_of_operating_the_mobile_barriers_in_Venice_MOSE_under_climate_change), [Boon and burden: economic performance and future perspectives of the Venice flood protection system](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10113-024-02193-9). By 2150, extreme scenarios suggest the barriers themselves could be submerged or rendered ineffective [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract).

sources.title: How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint
sources.content:
The Mo.S.E. (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barriers, a mobile flap gate system designed to protect Venice from flooding, face significant effectiveness challenges due to relative sea level rise (RSLR) and wind setup effects. 

Key Findings and Projections:
- **Failure Threshold:** The Mo.S.E. system is projected to fail in controlling flood events once relative sea level rise reaches **+0.40 m**. Beyond this level, the enhanced wind setup constraint prevents the barriers from protecting all urban settlements within the lagoon.
- **Wind Setup Impact:** During barrier closures, wind setup (the accumulation of water caused by wind blowing across the lagoon) can raise water levels inside the lagoon independently of the sea. This effect will increasingly result in long and repeated closures, complicating the balance between flood protection and lagoon health.
- **Closure Frequency and Duration:** Climate change is expected to 'rapidly increase' both the duration and frequency of barrier closures. This poses a management challenge for maintaining port and industrial activities, as well as the conservation of the lagoon ecosystem, which requires regular water exchange with the sea.
- **Management Optimization:** The study suggests a new strategy to optimize gate operations by identifying the shortest possible closure duration that still prevents flooding across all settlements, based on an analysis of the last 20 years of tidal records.
- **Contextual Vulnerability:** The Venice Lagoon is highlighted as one of the most vulnerable areas globally to the flooding exacerbations predicted in the latest IPCC reports due to its historical urban settlements and geography.

sources.title: Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy
sources.content:
### Sea Level Rise (SLR) Projections for Venice
- **Historical context:** Sea levels in Venice have risen by approximately 26 cm over the past century.
- **IPCC Scenarios (RCP):** Projections for changes in extreme total water levels include:
  - **RCP4.5 (by 2100):** 20 to 40 cm increase.
  - **RCP8.5 (by 2050):** 20 to 40 cm increase.
  - **RCP8.5 (by 2100):** Up to 80 cm increase.
- **Critical Thresholds:** 
  - 120 cm: St. Mark’s Square floods.
  - 140 cm: Footbridges are required to cross the square.
  - 160 cm: Water enters St. Mark’s Basilica.
  - 194 cm: Record height reached during the 'Great Flood' of November 4, 1966.

### MoSE Barrier Effectiveness and Performance
- **Operational Status:** The Experimental Electromechanical Module (MoSE) consists of mobile barriers at the three lagoon inlets (Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia). It was successfully tested in 2020 and is now operational.
- **Cost-Benefit Analysis:** 
  - Daily operational cost of MoSE is estimated at 0.025 million EUR (MEUR).
  - For events analogous to the 1966 extreme (194 cm), MoSE significantly reduced Sea Levels (SL) and lowered average estimated damages from 0.45 MEUR to 0.25 MEUR.
  - For 2008 and 2019 analogues, while MoSE reduced sea levels, the study found it was occasionally 'non cost-effective' for lower-intensity events where median damages with operational costs were higher than counterfactual scenarios without the barrier.
  - For the 2018 'Vaia' storm analogues, the analysis found no significant change in outcomes, potentially due to the specific atmospheric river dynamics that MoSE cannot fully mitigate if they involve complex rainfall-driven flooding in the lagoon.
- **System Efficacy:** The study confirms MoSE provides significant protection against the most extreme surges (analogous to 1966) by preventing Scirocco-driven Adriatic water from entering the lagoon. However, the system's effectiveness is challenged by the increasing frequency of events and the need for more frequent closures as sea levels rise.

### Flood Frequency and Attribution
- **Frequency Trends:** Acqua Alta events have become 'increasingly frequent and severe' in recent decades. The study attributes this primarily to human-driven climate change, which has altered atmospheric circulation patterns, such as the 'Genoa Low' and Scirocco wind intensity.
- **Drivers of Flooding:** Floods are a compound result of astronomical tides, storm surges (Scirocco winds pushing water up the Adriatic), and synoptic weather patterns like Mediterranean cyclones. Climate change exacerbates these by increasing the baseline sea level and potentially intensifying storm dynamics.
- **Event Analogues:** The study highlights four major events (1966, 2008, 2018, 2019). The 2019 event was particularly notable for being driven by a small-sized secondary cyclone that caused significant flooding (187 cm) despite being difficult to predict using large-scale models.

sources.title: The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change
sources.content:
The research, primarily based on the work of Umgiesser (2020) and subsequent hydrodynamic modeling, details the projected impacts of sea level rise (SLR) on Venice and the operational effectiveness of the MOSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barrier system.

### Sea Level Rise & Flood Statistics
*   **Current Rate:** The mean relative sea level in Venice has been rising at approximately 4.9 mm/year due to the combined effects of eustasy and land subsidence.
*   **Historical Trends:** Exceptional flood events (exceeding 140 cm) have increased in frequency: 23 such events occurred in the last 150 years, but 14 of those have occurred since 2000.
*   **IPCC Projections:** Based on IPCC AR6 (2021), a sea level rise between 42 cm and 78 cm is expected by the end of the 21st century.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness & Closure Frequency
The MOSE system consists of 78 mobile gates across four barriers at the lagoon inlets. They are typically raised when the water level is forecasted to reach or exceed the 110 cm safeguarding threshold, at which point 12% of Venice's historic center is flooded.

*   **At 30 cm SLR:** The number of hours where the sea surface height (SSH) exceeds 110 cm is projected to reach 3 to 30 hours per year (compared to a maximum of 6 hours in the 2000–2002 reference period).
*   **At 50 cm SLR:** The barriers will be required to close on average once per day. At this level, they can still keep the internal lagoon level below 110 cm nearly all the time.
*   **At 75 cm SLR:** The lagoon is projected to be closed for more hours than it is open.
*   **Scenario RCP 8.5 (High Emissions):** 
    *   By mid-century (around 2050), MOSE will need to be closed for more than 20% of the time during the months of October, November, and December.
    *   By the end of the century (2100), the lagoon is projected to remain closed for approximately 260 days per year.
*   **Scenario RCP 4.5:** Impacts are described as "much less severe" and "possibly much less challenging" for the ecosystem compared to RCP 8.5, though specific closure day counts for this scenario were not detailed in the summary text.

### Operational Findings
*   **Partial Closures:** Hydrodynamic simulations indicate that partial closures of the inlets are ineffective for flood defense. Only complete closure of all inlets guarantees a noticeable reduction in lagoon water levels.
*   **Flux Reduction:** A SLR of 50 cm results in a moderate 10% reduction in water flux at the inlets during open periods.

### Secondary Impacts of Frequent Closures
*   **Water Renewal:** Under the RCP 8.5 scenario at the end of the century, the average water renewal time for the lagoon is expected to triplicate. More than 35% of the lagoon will require more than 20 days to renew its water.
*   **Ecological Risks:** Prolonged closures lead to oxygen depletion (hypoxia), increased water temperatures (exceeding 30°C for over 4 months under RCP 8.5), and intensified marine heat waves (four times stronger in shallow tidal flats than in the open sea). This alters sediment fluxes, primary production, and the life cycles of lagoon communities.

sources.title: Boon and burden: economic performance and future perspectives of the Venice flood protection system
sources.content:
The MoSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) system is a network of 78 storm surge mobile barriers across the three inlets of Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia, designed to protect Venice from flooding. 

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Operations
- **Closure Threshold:** The official closure threshold is set at 110 cm above mean sea level. However, some studies and the current paper discuss the potential need to increase this threshold to manage the frequency of closures as sea levels rise.
- **Flood Protection:** At a water level of 90 cm, 2% of Venice is flooded. At 140 cm (the level of severe events), 59% of the city floods. At 200 cm, over 90% of the city is submerged. The MoSE system isolates the lagoon from the sea during these events to prevent such damages.
- **Residual Damage:** Even with MoSE active, residual damages occur in the lowest parts of the city (starting at 90 cm) before the barrier is fully deployed or if the threshold is reached.

### Projected Sea Level Rise (SLR) and Flood Frequency
- **Scenarios Used:** The study utilizes two SLR scenarios until 2100 based on the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (IPCC 2022) to assess the system's viability. While the specific numerical SLR values (in meters) for each scenario are integrated into a system dynamics model, the paper emphasizes that global mean sea level rose by 17 cm in the 20th century and continues to accelerate.
- **Flood Frequency Trends:** Extreme events (acqua alta) have increased in frequency and are predicted to grow significantly. The paper warns that an "excessive number of closures" due to SLR will create a "tipping point" challenging the infrastructure's functionality.
- **Ecological Impact:** Frequent closures disrupt water exchange and sediment accumulation on salt marshes, limiting the ecosystem's ability for vertical accretion to keep pace with SLR.

### Economic Impacts and Viability
- **Investment Cost:** The total investment for the MoSE project exceeds €6 billion.
- **Operational Costs:** Estimated in the order of several tens of millions of euros per year (including energy and labor).
- **Port Losses:** MoSE closures block access for vessels, causing revenue losses for the Port of Venice. Direct costs to ship traffic are estimated between €348,000 and €1.3 million annually. A "memory effect" is noted where vessels may avoid the port permanently due to frequent closure encounters.
- **CBA Findings:** Simulations suggest economic benefits (avoided real estate and commercial damage) generally outweigh investment and port revenue costs. However, the viability is threatened in the medium-to-long term as rising sea levels necessitate more frequent and prolonged closures.

### Historical Context
- **Record Floods:** The most severe flood reached 194 cm on November 4, 1966. A more recent major event occurred on November 12, 2019, reaching 187 cm.

sources.title: The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice
sources.content:
Venice is increasingly threatened by relative sea-level rise (RSL), which is a combination of global sea-level rise (SLR) and significant land subsidence (vertical land movement or VLM) caused by the compaction of soft sediment (clay and silt) and historical groundwater extraction. Projections for the next 100 years predict an acceleration in SLR for Venice due to thermal expansion and ice melt.

### MOSE Barrier System and Flood History
- **Structure:** The Experimental Electromechanical Module (MOSE) barrier consists of 78 mobile gates across three lagoon inlets: Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia. It was first used in 2020. 
- **Functionality:** The gates are situated on the inlet bed and close when high water levels are forecast. The Malamocco inlet includes a lock for larger vessels to maintain port access during closures.
- **Historical Flood Levels:** Water levels are measured relative to the Zero Mareografico Punta della Salute (ZMPS). Significant events include the 1966 flood (194 cm + ZMPS) which submerged the city under 1.5 meters of water, and the November 2019 flood (187 cm + ZMPS) which inundated St. Mark's Square and the Doge's Palace.

### Impact Projections and Barrier Effectiveness
- **Closure Frequency:** SLR is projected to significantly increase the frequency of MOSE barrier closures. Frequent closures threaten the ecological balance of the 550 km² lagoon (by halting tidal flow) and disrupt shipping and economic activities.
- **Scenario Analysis:** The research utilized the Rosner framework and Extreme Value Analysis (EVA) to evaluate adaptations under non-stationary conditions (where historical trends change due to climate change). The study specifically focuses on the number of closures as a metric for damages, multiplied by the costs associated with each closure event.
- **Adaptation Strategy Evaluated:** The study analyzed 'lifting the city' by 30 cm through the injection of seawater into a deep soil layer. 
- **Key Findings on Effectiveness:** 
  - The analysis concluded that the expected 'regrets' (monetary losses due to preparedness errors or damages) are smaller when choosing to adapt (e.g., lifting the city) across all SLR scenarios compared to not adapting.
  - The difference in regret between adapting and not adapting increases with higher levels of SLR.
  - Adaptation (like city-lifting) is necessary to keep the MOSE barrier functional and the city safe, as the current barrier will eventually reach a limit in how often it can realistically be closed without causing unacceptable ecological and economic harm.
  - The study suggests that lifting the city by 30 cm would provide more time to evaluate long-term alternatives to the MOSE barrier as sea levels continue to rise.

### IPCC and SLR Scenarios
The report incorporates IPCC projections (though specific numbers for SSP scenarios like SSP1-2.6 or SSP5-8.5 are analyzed in Chapters 4 and 5, the introductory sections establish that the barrier's current operational threshold will be exceeded under all projected SLR trends, necessitating secondary adaptations). The research emphasizes that even if global temperatures stabilize, sea levels will continue to rise for decades to millennia due to the high heat capacity of oceans.

sources.title: The projected MOSE barriers against flooding in Venice (Italy) and the expected global sea-level rise
sources.content:
### Projected Sea Level Rise (SLR) and Subsidence
- **Historical context:** Venice experienced 25 cm of Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR) in the 20th century (12 cm land subsidence + 13 cm eustatic rise). Historical tide gauge data (1890–2007) shows an average rise rate of 0.12 ± 0.01 cm/year.
- **Future Projections (2100):** RSLR is estimated to range between 17 cm and 53 cm by 2100. Global sea-level rise specifically is estimated at 48 cm by 2100.
- **Long-term (2150):** Under IPCC AR6 scenarios (SSP1-2.6 to SSP5-8.5), RSLR could reach extreme levels. In high-emission scenarios (SSP5-8.5), the RSLR plus extreme events (tides, seiches, surges) could temporarily reach 3.47 m above the Punta della Salute reference level by 2150.
- **Subsidence:** Current natural subsidence in Venice is approximately 0.05 cm/year, though some areas show Vertical Land Movement (VLM) of 1–3 mm/year, with peaks up to 7 mm/year.

### Flood Frequency Projections
- **Frequency Increase:** The frequency of tides >110 cm (the threshold for MOSE activation) is projected to increase from current levels (~4 times/year) to between 20 and 250 times per year by 2100.
- **Extreme Events:** Under a high-emission scenario, the magnitude of 1-in-100 year sea level events in the North Adriatic is projected to increase by 65% in 2050 and 160% in 2100 relative to present values.
- **Flooded Area:** By 2150, over 112 km² (low-emission) to 139 km² (high-emission) of the lagoon surface is at risk of permanent flooding. Extreme events could flood up to 65% of the land.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness
- **Operational Limitations:** MOSE is designed to protect against storm surges up to 3 meters. However, simulations suggest it cannot prevent flooding with a 50 cm SLR. Issues arise with as little as 20–30 cm of SLR.
- **Closure Threshold and Viability:** The official closure level is 110 cm. As RSLR increases, the required frequency of closures will rise, which threatens the economic viability of the port (due to maritime traffic delays) and the health of the lagoon ecosystem (due to restricted water exchange).
- **Failure Points:** If water levels rise 1 cm/h while gates are closed, internal lagoon levels can still exceed 110 cm for extended periods. In extreme 2100/2150 scenarios, the barrier itself could be submerged or rendered ineffective if mean sea levels rise above its design tolerance.
- **Operational Timing:** For maximum effectiveness in mitigating Extreme Sea Level (ESL) events, MOSE must be in full operation several hours before the event peak to control sea level fluctuations (inverse persistence).

sources.title: Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)
sources.content:
This study analyzes Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR) in the Venice Lagoon by combining Vertical Land Movement (VLM) data with IPCC 6th Assessment Report (AR6) global projections for Shared Socioeconomic Pathways SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5.

### Key Findings on Subsidence and Sea Level Rise (RSLR):
- **Vertical Land Movements:** Data from GNSS (1996–2023) and InSAR (2017–2023) show that while the ancient city of Venice is relatively stable, the three lagoon inlets where the MOSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barriers are located are experiencing significant subsidence of up to 2.9 mm/yr.
- **Projection Years:** The study calculates RSLR and flooding impacts for the years 2050, 2100, and 2150 AD.

### Flood Impacts and Land Loss by 2150:
- **Flooding Exposure:** By 2150, the land area at risk of flooding is projected to be between 112 km² (under the low-emission SSP1-2.6 scenario) and 159 km² (under the high-emission SSP5-8.5 scenario).
- **Extreme Water Levels:** When accounting for historical extreme events (astronomical tides, seiches, and atmospheric forcing) combined with the new RSLR projected for 2150, temporary water levels could reach up to 3.47 m.
- **Flood Percentage:** At a 3.47 m water level, up to 65% of the total lagoon land area may be flooded. In the lowest regions, such as Chioggia, approximately 90% of the land is expected to be covered by water.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness:
- The study highlights that these projected extreme scenarios (specifically the potential for 3.47 m water levels) raise critical questions regarding the future safety of lowland areas and the long-term durability and effectiveness of the MOSE artificial barrier system. The barriers are designed to protect the lagoon from high tides and flooding, but the accelerating RSLR and subsidence at the inlet locations may exceed the system's operational capacity or design life by 2150.

sources.title: Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon | PreventionWeb
sources.content:
This study analyzes future scenarios of relative sea level rise (RSLR) in the Venice Lagoon up to the year 2150, combining IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) climatic projections with localized land subsidence data (GNSS and InSAR). 

**Key Sea Level Rise Projections:**
- By 2150, the study projects a relative sea level rise of up to 153 cm under high-emission scenarios.
- Land subsidence is noted as highly variable across the lagoon, intensifying flood risks particularly near coastal and urban infrastructure.
- The study evaluates impacts across three Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs): SSP1-2.6 (low emissions), SSP3-7.0 (medium-high), and SSP5-8.5 (high emissions).

**Impacts on Land and Infrastructure:**
- Over 130 km² of land is threatened by 2150.
- Under the worst-case projections (SSP5-8.5) by 2150, up to 65% of the land in the lagoon area could be flooded.
- 11 key areas were specifically analyzed, including Venice city, industrial zones, the airport, and the MoSE flood barrier system locations.

**MoSE Barrier Effectiveness:**
- The MoSE barriers are designed to withstand water levels up to 3 meters.
- The study finds that these barriers may become insufficient by 2150, with the potential to be submerged during extreme events.
- High-resolution Digital Surface Models (DSMs) using LiDAR data indicate that the barriers and other critical structures face increased vulnerability as RSLR increases, potentially compromising their primary function of flood protection for the UNESCO World Heritage Site.

evaluation.url_relevance_rationale: Most URLs are directly about Venice sea-level rise, flood projections, and MOSE performance, including primary or near-primary research articles on MOSE effectiveness under climate change and IPCC-linked scenarios (e.g., Nature, ScienceDirect, Springer). However, a few sources are weaker fits or less authoritative for this specific query, such as ResearchGate/Academia reposts and the PreventionWeb summary rather than the underlying paper.
evaluation.tailored_comprehensiveness_rationale: Several extracts contain concrete query-specific details: scenario-linked sea-level rise values, flood thresholds, projected exceedance frequencies, and MOSE operational limits like +0.40 m failure, daily closure at +0.50 m, and ~260 closure days/year under RCP8.5. Still, not all extracts provide flood frequency projections under clearly matched IPCC scenarios, and some are generic about future risk or economics without the exact scenario-specific flood-frequency evidence the query asks for.
evaluation.synthesis_quality_rationale: The synthesis is largely grounded in the extracted content and answers both parts of the query: Venice-specific impacts and MOSE effectiveness under scenarios. I do not see major fabricated numbers, but there is some stitching together of heterogeneous scenario frameworks (RCPs, SSPs, and generic SLR thresholds) and a few broad framing claims like a system 'viability tipping point' that are more interpretive than directly evidenced as scenario-specific flood-frequency conclusions.

query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis:
Venice is already highly exposed because its flood problem is driven primarily by **relative** sea-level rise: the combination of global mean sea-level rise and local land subsidence. A 2021 review in *Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences* states that the historic increase in Venice flood frequency since the mid-19th century is explained by relative mean sea-level rise, and that future relative sea-level rise will be the most important driver of increasing flood duration and intensity through this century, outweighing the small projected decrease in storminess. In that review, for the northern Adriatic coast under **RCP8.5**, the **1-in-100-year water level** is projected to increase by **26–35 cm by 2050** and **53–171 cm by 2100** relative to present; under **RCP4.5**, the increase is **12–17 cm by 2050** and **24–56 cm by 2100**. The same paper notes local subsidence is not included in those particular numbers, so Venice-specific relative impacts can be worse if subsidence continues. [Nat. Hazards Earth Syst. Sci., 21, 2705–2731, 2021](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2705/2021/nhess-21-2705-2021.pdf)

IPCC AR6 WGII uses Venice as a case study and summarizes the issue as one of rising relative sea level plus rapidly increasing flood frequency. Its Figure Box 13.1.1 describes: observed evolution of relative and mean sea level in Venice; the **decadal frequency of floods above the safeguard level in the city centre**; projected **relative sea-level rise at the Venetian coast** from AR6 WG1; and the **timing when critical relative sea-level thresholds will be reached depending on scenarios**, including an upper bound under **SSP5-8.5** that represents a low-likelihood, high-impact storyline involving ice-sheet instability. IPCC Chapter 13 also explicitly says that **limits to adaptation** have been identified for coastal defenses such as the **Venice MoSE barrier**. [Figure AR6 WG2 | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/figures/chapter-13/figure-13-box-13-1-1/) [Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/chapter/chapter-13/)

A Venice-lagoon hydrodynamic study in *Frontiers in Climate* gives one of the clearest event-frequency projections tied to climate scenarios. Using present-day and 2050 scenarios, it reports **16 acqua alta events** in the control 2020 simulation, rising to **34** under a simple trend extrapolation for 2050, **36** under **RCP2.6-2050**, **42** under **RCP4.5-2050**, and **54** under **RCP8.5-2050**. For the most severe 2050 case they tested, **RCP8.5-2050**, mean sea level rises by **0.22 m**, the number of high-water events **triplicates to 54**, and **9** of those are **exceptional floods** with peak sea level of **1.4 m**, enough to flood about **60% of Venice**. [Frontiers | Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full)

That same study also quantifies how often MoSE would need to operate in those scenarios. Estimated annual **MoSE closure hours** rise from **357 hours (3% of the year)** in the 2020 control to **917 hours (7%)** in the trend-2050 case, **1,008 hours (8%)** under **RCP2.6-2050**, **1,097 hours (9%)** under **RCP4.5-2050**, and **1,324 hours (11%)** under **RCP8.5-2050**. The authors conclude that sea-level rise could increase closure time by **up to four times** relative to today. [Frontiers | Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full)

On effectiveness, the same *Frontiers* study says MoSE remains effective at **protecting Venice from severe flooding during storm-surge events** in the 2050 scenarios it modeled, keeping maximum water levels in the city **below 1.2 m even in climate-change scenarios**. So under roughly mid-century conditions, including **RCP8.5-2050**, MoSE still functions as a strong event-scale defense, but at the cost of much more frequent closures. [Frontiers | Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full)

A more general MOSE-operability study by Umgiesser modeled sea-level rise from **0 to 200 cm** and found a sharper long-term limit. It concludes the barriers can keep lagoon water below the **110 cm safeguard level** **nearly all the time if sea-level rise does not exceed 50 cm**. But at **50 cm SLR**, the barriers would be closed **on average once a day**; at **75 cm SLR**, the lagoon would be **closed more hours than open** on average; and with **100 cm SLR**, annual flooding time rises to **close to 1000 hours**, meaning MOSE would no longer be able to defend Venice adequately from high tides. The paper also finds **partial closures are ineffective** for city flood defense; a **complete closure** is needed for a noticeable reduction in lagoon water levels. [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://venezia2021.corila.it/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/1-s2.0-S1617138119302079-main.pdf)

A separate flood-risk study in *NHESS* reinforces the partial-failure point. It compared a fully functioning MOSE system with a scenario where all inlets except **Lido** close. For sea-level-rise scenarios of **0.15 m** and **0.45 m** above 2019 conditions, a partially functioning MOSE system still reduced average flood depth for **90% of buildings**, but by the highest tested scenario (**SLR2**, 0.45 m above 2019), sea-level rise increasingly dominated the protective effect. The authors conclude Venice will likely continue facing flood risk despite MOSE, especially under partial failure and higher sea levels. [NHESS - Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/22/2381/2022/nhess-22-2381-2022.html)

Recent Italian work extending AR6-style scenario analysis to 2150 indicates that the engineering margin is tight even if MOSE continues to operate. INGV summarizes a 2024 study saying MOSE was designed to protect against a sea–lagoon level difference of up to **3 m** and a **mean sea level of 60 cm in 2100**, but that this design envelope **could be exceeded toward the end of this century** under critical scenarios. The study used **IPCC-based SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5** pathways together with measured subsidence to map flooding for **2050, 2100, and 2150**. [CLIMATE CHANGE | Possible sea level rise scenarios highlighted in Venice](https://www.ingv.it/en/urp-press/Press-office/Press-releases/Climate-change-highlights-possible-sea-level-rise-scenarios-in-Venice) [Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon | PreventionWeb](https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/multi-temporal-relative-sea-level-rise-scenarios-2150-venice-lagoon)

**Bottom line by scenario:** under **lower to intermediate IPCC pathways** around mid-century (roughly **RCP2.6/RCP4.5** or similar SSPs), Venice is projected to see a marked rise in flood frequency, but MoSE still appears capable of preventing most severe event flooding if fully functional, albeit with substantially more closures. Under **high-end late-century pathways** such as **RCP8.5 / SSP5-8.5**, flood frequency and extreme-water-level increases become much larger, closure frequency rises toward operational impracticality, and multiple studies plus the IPCC indicate Venice approaches or exceeds the adaptation limits of the current barrier system. [Frontiers | Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full) [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://venezia2021.corila.it/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/1-s2.0-S1617138119302079-main.pdf) [Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/chapter/chapter-13/)

One important caveat is that the sources do **not** all report scenario results in the same framework. Some Venice studies still use **RCP2.6/4.5/8.5**, while newer IPCC-consistent work and the latest Venice-lagoon mapping use **SSP1-2.6/SSP3-7.0/SSP5-8.5**. The overall message is consistent across them: relative sea-level rise sharply increases flood frequency, MoSE is effective in the near to mid term if fully operational, but its usefulness declines as sea level approaches roughly **50–60 cm** of rise and becomes severely constrained under high-end late-century outcomes. [Figure AR6 WG2 | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/figures/chapter-13/figure-13-box-13-1-1/) [The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change](https://venezia2021.corila.it/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/1-s2.0-S1617138119302079-main.pdf) [CLIMATE CHANGE | Possible sea level rise scenarios highlighted in Venice](https://www.ingv.it/en/urp-press/Press-office/Press-releases/Climate-change-highlights-possible-sea-level-rise-scenarios-in-Venice)

sources.title: Nat. Hazards Earth Syst. Sci., 21, 2705–2731, 2021
sources.content: Review paper on Venice flooding and sea level. States the historic increase in flood frequency since the mid-19th century is explained by relative mean sea-level rise. Says future regional relative mean sea-level rise will be the most important driver of increasing duration and intensity of Venice floods through this century, despite a small projected decrease in marine storminess. For high-emission RCP8.5, the magnitude of 1-in-100-year water height values at the northern Adriatic coast is projected to increase by 26–35 cm by 2050 and by 53–171 cm by 2100 relative to present. For moderate-emission RCP4.5, projected increases are 12–17 cm by 2050 and 24–56 cm by 2100. The summary notes local subsidence is not included in these particular values. Also states RSL rise has been the dominant factor responsible for the significant increase in frequency of floods of the Venice city centre.

sources.title: Frontiers | Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice
sources.content: Hydrodynamic study of present and future sea level extremes in the regulated Venice Lagoon. Reports that with a rise of mean sea level of 0.22 m, as predicted in the RCP8.5 scenario for 2050, the number of acqua alta events will triplicate to 54 in total, including 9 exceptional floods, defined as peak sea level reaching 1.4 m and flooding about 60% of Venice. Table 3 gives event and closure counts: CTRL-2020: 16 acqua alta events, 357 hours of MoSE closure (3% of year); TREND-2050: 34 events, 917 h closure (7%); RCP2.6-2050: 36 events, 1,008 h closure (8%); RCP4.5-2050: 42 events, 1,097 h closure (9%); RCP8.5-2050: 54 events, 1,324 h closure (11%). States sea-level rise will increase lagoon-sea exchange by almost 10% in RCP8.5-2050 absent closures, but future relative sea-level rise will increase hours the MoSE needs to be closed by up to four times. Concludes the MoSE is effective in protecting Venice from severe flooding during storm-surge events and keeps maximum sea water levels below 1.2 m even in climate change scenarios tested.

sources.title: The impact of operating the mobile barriers in Venice (MOSE) under climate change
sources.content: Umgiesser 2020, Journal for Nature Conservation. Uses a 2D hydrodynamic model to simulate MOSE operations under sea-level rise from 0 to 200 cm. Abstract states barriers can keep water level below the 110 cm safeguarding level nearly all the time if SLR does not exceed 50 cm. With 50 cm SLR, barriers would be closed on average once a day; with 75 cm SLR, the lagoon would be closed more hours than open on average. Flux reduction at inlets is moderate, about 10% for 50 cm SLR. Partial closures are ineffective for Venice flood defense; only complete closure guarantees a noticeable reduction of water levels in the lagoon. Paper notes Venice is on average only 80 cm above mean sea level. Figure discussion says with 50 cm SLR St. Mark’s Square is flooded nearly half of the time without protection. Discussion says without operating the mobile gates, Venice would experience flooding for more than 1000 hours when sea level rises by 50 cm. With closures, even at 50 cm SLR exceedance of the safeguarding level is only 18 h/year in one forecast setup or 100 h/year in a worse case. But with 100 cm SLR, annual flooding time is close to 1000 h, so MOSE would no longer be able to defend Venice from high tides.

sources.title: NHESS - Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy
sources.content: Flood-risk framework paper comparing all-open, all-closed, and partial MOSE closure scenarios. Uses sea-level-rise scenarios of 0.15 m and 0.45 m with respect to 2019. Describes two closure states: fully functioning MOSE barrier ('allclosed') and a critical partial-failure scenario where all inlets except the Lido inlet close ('lidoopen'). Finds partial non-closure of Lido is likely the most critical partial-closure scenario. Reports that partially functioning MOSE significantly reduces expected average flood depth for 90% of buildings for lower sea-level-rise scenarios (SLR0 and SLR1), but in SLR2 the increased sea level dominates over the dampening effect of partial closure. Notes that during the 12 November 2019 storm surge, 50% of all structures in Venice experienced flood depth of 0.55 m or higher; only 10% of buildings had flood depth lower than 0.10 m; only 5% were not flooded at all. Concludes Venice is likely to continue facing flooding despite MOSE and that the framework can estimate future damage under partially failing MOSE barriers.

sources.title: Figure AR6 WG2 | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability
sources.content: IPCC AR6 WGII Figure Box 13.1.1 on Venice sea-level rise and coastal flooding. Caption says panel (a) shows evolution of relative and mean sea level in Venice and decadal frequency of floods above the safeguard level in the city centre; panel (b) shows projected relative sea-level rise at the Venetian coast based on Fox-Kemper et al. 2021; 'very likely' corresponds to 5–95th percentile range and 'likely' to 17–83rd percentile range; panel (c) shows timing when critical relative sea-level thresholds will be reached depending on scenarios and confidence level. It specifically says the upper limit of the medium confidence range under SSP5-8.5 represents a low-likelihood, high-impact storyline and that low-confidence processes include ice-sheet instability. Panel (d) is a Landsat view of Venice and the lagoon inlets.

sources.title: Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability
sources.content: IPCC AR6 WGII Europe chapter. States that without further adaptation, flood risks along Europe’s low-lying coasts and estuaries will increase due to sea-level rise compounded by storm surges, rainfall and runoff. Explicitly says limits to adaptation to extremely high sea-level-rise scenarios have been identified for coastal defences such as the Venice MoSE barrier, Thames Barrier, and Maeslant Barrier.

sources.title: CLIMATE CHANGE | Possible sea level rise scenarios highlighted in Venice
sources.content: INGV press release summarizing a 2024 study titled 'Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)' published in Remote Sensing. Says the study analyzed up-to-date IPCC projections and geodetic data to estimate surfaces exposed to flooding in coming decades due to sea-level rise. States MOSE is currently designed to protect Venice from high waters up to a height difference of 3 meters between open sea and lagoon and a mean sea level of 60 cm in 2100, but under critical scenarios this could be exceeded toward the end of this century. Explains the study combines GNSS, SAR, tide-gauge time series, and high-resolution topographic data, and provides projected sea levels and maps of possible flood scenarios for 2050, 2100, and 2150.

sources.title: Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon | PreventionWeb
sources.content: Summary page for the 2024 Venice-lagoon study. States the goal is to evaluate how global sea-level rise and localized land subsidence will affect flooding in 11 key areas of the lagoon, including Venice city, industrial zones, airport, and the MOSE barrier system. Says the study builds high-resolution DSMs using LiDAR data to map projected flooding under three SSPs: SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5, with maps for 2050, 2100, and 2150.

evaluation.url_relevance_rationale: Most URLs are strong primary or near-primary sources for this query: Venice-specific peer-reviewed studies on flood frequency and MOSE performance (Sources 1–4), plus IPCC AR6 materials directly addressing Venice and adaptation limits (Sources 5–6). The weaker links are Source 7 (a press release) and Source 8 (a summary page), which are relevant but not ideal primary evidence for the requested scenario-specific impacts.
evaluation.tailored_comprehensiveness_rationale: Several extracts contain concrete, query-specific numbers: flood-event counts and MOSE closure hours by scenario in Source 2, extreme-water-level increases under RCP4.5/RCP8.5 in Source 1, and MOSE operability thresholds under 50/75/100 cm SLR in Source 3. However, the IPCC extracts are mostly descriptive rather than numeric, and the later SSP-based sources do not provide Venice-specific flood-frequency figures or detailed MOSE effectiveness metrics in the extract.
evaluation.synthesis_quality_rationale: The synthesis is mostly grounded in the extracts and uses many traceable numbers correctly, especially from Sources 1–3. But it also makes some interpretive claims that are not directly supported by the provided extracts, such as 'MoSE still appears capable of preventing most severe event flooding' under lower/intermediate pathways and especially the final statement that usefulness 'declines as sea level approaches roughly 50–60 cm of rise,' which blends Source 3's 50 cm threshold with Source 7's design mean sea level of 60 cm rather than citing a direct extracted conclusion.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) transitioned the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (BFT-E) stock from a recovery plan to a rule-based Management Procedure (MP) during the 2022–2026 period. The Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas and their scientific foundations are summarized below:

### 2022 TAC: 36,000 tonnes
*   **Scientific Basis:** This quota was established under Recommendation 21-08, marking the final phase of the transition from the previous multiannual recovery plan (Rec. 17-07) to a new management framework [Report of the 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment Meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf), [ICCAT Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf).
*   **Stock Assessment Results:** The 2022 assessment utilized three modeling platforms: Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and the Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP). All models showed a strong increase in Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB) since the late 2000s [BFT-E: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Executive Summary (2024)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf). However, model results on overfishing status were contradictory: the VPA indicated overfishing was occurring (F > F0.1), while SS and ASAP indicated it was not [Report of the 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment Meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf).

### 2023–2025 TAC: 40,570 tonnes (per year)
*   **Scientific Basis:** These TACs were the first established using the newly adopted Management Procedure (Rec. 22-09), a formulaic framework developed through Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) [2024 SCRS Report - Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf), [Atlantic Bluefin Tuna Fisheries 2025 Fishery to Date](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/s3/2025-09/BFT-Year-in-Review-Fall-AP-2025-Final-presentation-508-compliant-.pdf). The MP uses pre-agreed rules and abundance indices to set TACs automatically, including a stability clause that limits the magnitude of annual quota changes [2024 SCRS Report - Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf).
*   **Stock Assessment Results:** The 2022 non-update assessment results were used to calibrate the MP. An integrated point estimate of fishing mortality (FCURRENT/F0.1 = 0.81) indicated that overfishing was not occurring [BFT-E: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Executive Summary (2024)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf). In 2024 and 2025, the Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) reviewed abundance indices and determined that no "Exceptional Circumstances" existed to warrant deviating from the MP-calculated TAC [BFT-E: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Executive Summary (2024)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf), [ICCAT Recommendation 25-05](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-05-e.pdf).

### 2026 TAC: 48,403 tonnes
*   **Scientific Basis:** In November 2025, the Commission applied the next cycle of the Management Procedure (2026–2028), resulting in a 19.3% increase in the TAC [ICCAT Press Release - 29th Regular Meeting of the Commission (November 2025)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf). The decision was based on the successful long-term rebuilding of the stock and updated scientific indicators processed through the MP [EU secures 17% increase of bluefin tuna fishing quota at ICCAT annual meeting](https://oceans-and-fisheries.ec.europa.eu/news/eu-secures-17-increase-bluefin-tuna-fishing-quota-iccat-annual-meeting-2025-11-27_en).
*   **Stock Assessment Results:** While a full stock assessment is scheduled for 2026 or 2027, the 2025 adjustment was driven by MP rules rather than a new full assessment [BFT-E: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Executive Summary (2024)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf), [ICCAT Press Release - 29th Regular Meeting of the Commission (November 2025)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf). Reports indicated the stock is "thriving" due to decades of strict conservation and rebuilding efforts [EU secures 17% increase of bluefin tuna fishing quota at ICCAT annual meeting](https://oceans-and-fisheries.ec.europa.eu/news/eu-secures-17-increase-bluefin-tuna-fishing-quota-iccat-annual-meeting-2025-11-27_en).

### Data Gaps and Uncertainties
Despite the positive trends, scientific reports highlight ongoing uncertainties regarding illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing, recruitment variability since 2011 due to retrospective bias, and the exact absolute biomass of the stock [BFT-E: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Executive Summary (2024)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf), [Report of the 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment Meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf).

sources.title: Report of the 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment Meeting
sources.content:
The provided document is the report of the 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E) Stock Assessment Meeting, held from July 4–9, 2022. This document provides the scientific basis and stock assessment results used to inform management decisions, although specific TAC quotas for 2023–2026 were to be finalized at subsequent meetings (September 2022 Species Group meeting and the November 2022 Commission meeting).

### Scientific Basis and Modeling
Three assessment modeling platforms were used to evaluate the stock status:
- **Virtual Population Analysis (VPA-2Box):** Updated from the 2017 base case. It incorporated revised size distributions for the 'inflated catch' period (1998–2007) and key indices like the Western Mediterranean larval survey (WMED_LARV).
- **Stock Synthesis (SS):** Assumed a Richards growth function with LINF fixed at 271 cm. It fitted data from 1950–2020 across 16 fishing fleets and 11 indices of abundance.
- **Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP):** A statistical catch-at-age model fitted to time series of catch, age composition, and indices. It explored both single-fleet and multi-fleet implementations.

### Stock Assessment Results (2022)
- **Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB):** All three models showed similar trends, with a sharp decline between the 1970s and 1990s, followed by a strong and steady increase in SSB since the late 2000s/2010. Stock Synthesis showed a terminal year (2020) harvest rate of approximately 7% of total biomass.
- **Recruitment:** Estimates indicate a clear increase in recent years, with strong recruitment years identified (e.g., 1994 and 2003). However, high uncertainty exists for recruitment estimates since 2011 due to retrospective bias.
- **Fishing Mortality (F):** F peaked in the late 1990s/early 2000s but dropped significantly in 2007 following new regulations and improved monitoring. 
- **Stock Status (Relative to F0.1 reference level):**
  - **VPA:** FCURRENT/F0.1 = 1.16 (Indicating that overfishing was occurring).
  - **Stock Synthesis:** FCURRENT/F0.1 = 0.72 (Indicating overfishing was NOT occurring).
  - **ASAP:** FCURRENT/F0.1 = 0.542 (Indicating overfishing was NOT occurring).

### Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE)
Management decisions for the 2023–2026 period transitioned toward the use of a Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) framework. The Group reviewed Candidate Management Procedures (CMPs) that incorporated requests from Panel 2. These procedures are designed to set TACs automatically based on pre-agreed rules (Management Procedures) rather than traditional ad-hoc assessments.

### TAC and Decision Context
- **2022 TAC:** While the assessment was conducted in 2022, the TAC for 2022 had been previously set (36,000 t under Rec. 21-08).
- **2023–2026 TACs:** The document states that "the Group decided to discuss further the management advice at the Species Group meeting in September 2022." (Note: Following this meeting and the MSE adoption, ICCAT Recommendation 22-08 eventually set the TAC for 2023, 2024, and 2025 at 40,570 t each year).

sources.title: BFT-E: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Executive Summary (2024)
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022-2026)
- **2023–2025:** The TAC for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna is set at **40,570 t** per year. This was established under Recommendation 22-08, based on the newly adopted Management Procedure (MP).
- **2022:** The reported catch was 35,110 t. The document notes that catches for recent years (2019–2022) stabilized around established TAC levels (which were 36,000 t for 2020-2022 under previous Rec. 21-08, though the 36,000 t figure is represented visually in BFT-E-Figure 1).
- **2026:** A specific TAC for 2026 is not explicitly listed in this document; however, the Committee notes that the next stock assessment is scheduled for **2026 or 2027**, and the MP provides the mechanism for future TAC advice without requiring annual assessments.

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
- **Management Procedure (MP) Transition (Rec. 22-09):** Adopted in 2022, the MP represents a foundational shift to a framework that links Eastern and Western area TACs. It uses simulation-tested rules to achieve MSY-based management objectives, freeing the assessment process from providing annual TAC advice and accounting for uncertainties in stock mixing and recruitment.
- **2022 Stock Assessment Results:**
  - **Modelling Platforms:** Three platforms were used: Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP).
  - **Fishing Mortality (F):** The integrated point estimate for fishing mortality relative to the reference level was **FCURRENT/F0.1 = 0.81** (95% CI: 0.48–1.62). This indicates the stock is **not overfishing**.
  - **Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB):** All models showed a progressive decline from the 1970s until the 2006 Recovery Plan (Rec. 06-05), followed by a **strong increase in SSB** since the late 2000s.
  - **Recruitment:** Two distinct periods were identified: low recruitment before 1990 and higher (though highly variable/uncertain) recruitment thereafter.
- **Current Stock Status (as of 2020 data):**
  - **Overfishing:** No.
  - **Overfished:** Not explicitly stated as "no" due to uncertainty in absolute biomass, but the Committee uses F0.1 as a proxy. The stock status determination remains based on the 2022 assessment until the next cycle.
- **Exceptional Circumstances (EC):** In 2024, the Committee evaluated updated indices of abundance (Rec. 23-07) and determined that **no Exceptional Circumstances existed** that would warrant deviating from the MP-calculated TAC for 2025.
- **Abundance Indices:** The 2022 assessment used 10 indices (7 CPUE series and 3 fisheries-independent indices). The MP currently utilizes 5 specific indices for the Eastern management area (2 CPUE indices and 3 surveys).
- **Uncertainties:** Key concerns include unquantified illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing, environmental factors affecting abundance indices, and uncertainty in total population scale. To address these, a Close-Kin Mark-Recapture (CKMR) Implementation Plan and a 2025 workplan have been proposed.

sources.title: 2024 SCRS Report - Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2022–2026)

Based on the Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) and the adoption of Management Procedures (MP), the following TACs were established:
- **2022:** 36,000 t (as per Rec. 21-08).
- **2023:** 40,570 t (determined by the first application of the Management Procedure, MP).
- **2024:** 40,570 t.
- **2025:** 40,570 t.
- **2026:** Subject to the next cycle of the Management Procedure application (typically set in 3-year blocks; the current 40,570 t TAC applies for the 2023-2025 period).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results

**1. Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) and Management Procedure (MP):**
In 2022, the Commission adopted a Management Procedure (Rec. 22-09) to set TACs for bluefin tuna. This marked a shift from traditional 'best assessment' methods to a rule-based approach tested against a wide range of uncertainties. The MP (specifically 'Candidate Management Procedure' or CMP) was selected to ensure a high probability of maintaining the stock in the 'Green Zone' of the Kobe plot (B > Bmsy and F < Fmsy).

**2. 2022 Stock Assessment:**
A non-update stock assessment was conducted in 2022 using various models (VPA and SS3). The results indicated:
- **Biomass Trends:** Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB) showed a significant increase since the mid-2000s, following the implementation of the recovery plan.
- **Stock Status:** The stock was likely not overfished and not undergoing overfishing, though exact Bmsy and Fmsy values remain uncertain due to different recruitment hypotheses.
- **Scientific Advice:** The SCRS recommended that the MP be the primary basis for TAC advice. The MP was designed to handle the 'regime shift' uncertainty (whether recruitment potential is high or low).

**3. Key Assessment Results (from 2024 review):**
- **Recent Catches:** Total catches in 2023 were 40,111 t, which is close to the 40,570 t TAC.
- **Abundance Indices:** Recent fishery-independent indices (e.g., aerial surveys, acoustic surveys, and larval surveys) generally show stable or increasing trends in the Mediterranean and Eastern Atlantic.
- **Recruitment:** Recent recruitment (2018–2022) appears to be above the long-term average, though there is high variability.

### Decision-Making Rationale
- **For 2023-2025:** The TAC of 40,570 t was calculated using the adopted Management Procedure. The MP uses data from abundance indices (e.g., Western Mediterranean larval index, French aerial survey, and others) to adjust the TAC every three years. The stability of the 40,570 t figure reflects the MP’s 'stability clause' which limits the maximum increase or decrease in TAC to ensure industry predictability while maintaining biological sustainability.
- **Scientific Uncertainty:** The SCRS continues to monitor 'Exceptional Circumstances.' If indicators (like the 2024 indices) fall outside the range tested in the MSE, the SCRS may recommend deviating from the MP. In 2024, no such circumstances were triggered for BFT-E.

sources.title: Atlantic Bluefin Tuna Fisheries 2025 Fishery to Date - HMS Advisory Panel Meeting September 2025
sources.content:
Based on the ICCAT management summaries provided in the document for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean (EBFT) bluefin tuna stock:

### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas
*   **2023–2025:** The annual TAC for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean stock is set at **40,570 metric tons (mt)**. This quota is governed by **ICCAT Recommendation 24-05** and involves 17 parties, including the European Union.
*   **2026:** The specific TAC for 2026 has not yet been set. ICCAT's scientific body (SCRS) was anticipated to provide Management Procedure (MP)-based TAC advice for the **2026–2028 management cycle** in the fall of 2025.
*   **2022:** While the document focuses on the 2023–2025 cycle, it notes that the current Management Procedure (MP) establishing these TACs was adopted in 2022 (Rec. 22-09) and implemented starting in 2023.

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
*   **Management Procedure (MP):** Following years of development, ICCAT adopted a Management Procedure in 2022 (Rec. 22-09, updated in Rec. 23-07). This MP is used to establish TACs for the management areas based on a predefined set of rules to ensure long-term sustainability. The 2023–2025 TAC of 40,570 mt was the first set under this MP-based approach.
*   **Stock Status:** As of the **2021 stock assessment**, ICCAT concluded that **no overfishing is occurring** for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna stock. The "overfished" status (whether the biomass is below a certain threshold) was not formally assessed by ICCAT in that cycle.
*   **Future Assessments:** The next full stock assessment for bluefin tuna is currently anticipated to take place in **2026**.
*   **Governance:** TAC and quota recommendations are first endorsed by members of ICCAT's **Panel 2** (which manages temperate tunas) and then adopted by the full ICCAT commission via consensus.

sources.title: ICCAT Recommendation 25-05: Conservation and Management Plan for Western Atlantic Bluefin Tuna
sources.content:
The provided document is Recommendation 25-05, which replaces Recommendation 22-10 and establishes a conservation and management plan for Western Atlantic bluefin tuna. While the research query focuses on Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (BFT-E), this document primarily details Western Atlantic quotas but includes several specific facts and the scientific basis relevant to the broader management of both stocks under the Management Procedure (MP) adopted in 2022.

### Quotas and Catch Limits (Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean)
- **Eastern TAC Contributions:** The document specifies that bycatch allocations for certain Western Atlantic fisheries are derived from the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna TAC. Specifically:
  - **United States:** 62.5 t per year allocated for bycatch in longline fisheries near the management area boundary.
  - **Canada:** 37.5 t per year allocated for the same purpose.
- **Total TAC for BFT-E:** The document does not list the total annual TAC for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean stock for 2022–2026, though it confirms that a Management Procedure (MP) was adopted in 2022 (Rec. 22-09/23-07) to establish TACs for both areas starting in 2023.

### Quotas (Western Atlantic) for Context
- **2026, 2027, and 2028:** An annual TAC of 3,081.6 t (inclusive of dead discards) is established for each of these years.
- **2029–2031:** TACs will be established at the 2028 ICCAT Annual Meeting based on the application of the MP.

### Scientific Basis and Decision-Making
- **Management Procedure (MP):** The TACs for both management areas (starting in 2023) are established based on a Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) framework developed by the Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS). 
- **MSE Framework:** Used to test Candidate Management Procedures (CMPs) to demonstrate tradeoffs between management objectives: stock status, safety, stability, and yield. It examined 2- and 3-year management cycles.
- **Scientific Assessment (SCRS 2025):** The SCRS assessed the occurrence of "exceptional circumstances" in 2025 but was unable to reach a consensus on their existence. 
- **TAC Calculation Logic:** According to the MP, when exceptional circumstances are not confirmed, a specific calculation (referred to as "BR") is used. For the Western stock, the BR calculation set a TAC of 2,568 t. However, the Commission adopted a TAC of 3,081.6 t, which represents 120% of the calculated amount (the maximum increase allowed before triggering the exceptional circumstance protocol).
- **Exceptional Circumstances Protocol:** Defined in Annex 4 of Rec. 23-07, it stipulates that catch exceeding 120% of the MP-produced TAC would constitute an exceptional circumstance.

### Scientific Research Initiatives (Supporting Basis)
ICCAT has identified five strategic initiatives to support the MP and future stock assessments:
1. Coordination of bluefin tuna (BFT) tagging.
2. Coordination of larval surveys.
3. Coordination of BFT biological sampling.
4. Coordination of population size and mixing estimation through genetic Close-Kin Mark-Recapture (CKMR).
5. Identification of environmental variability sources affecting stock productivity.

Other research focuses include genetic samples for stock identification, age and growth estimation, and electronic tagging to monitor migrations and mixing rates between the Eastern and Western management areas.

sources.title: ICCAT Press Release - 29th Regular Meeting of the Commission (November 2025)
sources.content:
The 29th Regular Meeting of the International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT), held in Seville in November 2025, established the following quotas and scientific bases for bluefin tuna:

- **Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna TAC (2026-2028):** The Commission set a new Total Allowable Catch (TAC) of **48,403 t** for the period 2026-2028. This represents a **19.3% increase** compared to the previous TAC.
- **Western Atlantic Bluefin Tuna TAC (2026-2028):** The TAC was set at **3,081.6 t**, which is a **13% increase** from the previous period.
- **Scientific Basis and Assessment Results:** The TAC decisions for both the western and eastern bluefin tuna stocks were based on the **"current management procedure"** (MP). Management procedures are formulaic frameworks (developed through Management Strategy Evaluation) that set TACs based on specific scientific indicators. While full scientific stock assessments were conducted in 2025 for other species (bigeye tuna, white marlin, and Atlantic shortfin mako shark), the bluefin tuna TACs were adjusted according to the pre-agreed management procedure rules.
- **Management Framework:** The decision for the eastern stock amends **Recommendation 24-05**, which established the multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean. The western stock decision amends **Recommendation 22-10**.
- **Skipjack Tuna Management:** For the first time, ICCAT agreed to a management procedure for western Atlantic skipjack tuna, setting a constant TAC of **30,844 t** for 2026-2028.
- **Shark Conservation:** The Commission adopted a new measure for southern Atlantic shortfin mako shark with a maximum mortality objective of **1,000 t**, and prohibited the retention, transshipment, or landing of basking sharks and great white sharks.
- **Compliance and Pilot Projects:** A pilot project was adopted to assess the conditions for fishing and farming bluefin tuna and yellowfin tuna in FAO zone 34.1 (Canary Islands).

sources.title: EU secures 17% increase of bluefin tuna fishing quota at ICCAT annual meeting
sources.content:
According to the ICCAT annual meeting concluded on November 24, 2025, the following quotas and scientific details were established for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna:

- **Total Allowable Catch (TAC) for 2026-2028:** ICCAT set a TAC of **48,403 tonnes per year** for the next three years (covering 2026, 2027, and 2028). This represents a significant increase from previous levels.
- **EU Specific Quota:** The European Union's quota for Eastern and Mediterranean bluefin tuna was increased by 3,661 tonnes (a 17% increase), reaching a total of **25,164 tonnes per year** for the 2026-2028 period.
- **Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment:**
  - The decision was based on a proposal from the European Union following "years of science-based decisions and strict controls."
  - The TAC increase reflects successful efforts over the past two decades to recover the stock. In 2007, the stock was close to depletion, leading to the introduction of a multiannual recovery plan.
  - Current assessments indicate that the bluefin tuna is "thriving once again" and the stock has been rebuilt due to "strict conservation measures, robust control, and science-based management."
  - The increase is described as a move toward more stable, higher quotas resulting from trusting the conservation process.

Note: The provided source specifically details the outcomes of the November 2025 meeting for the upcoming three-year cycle (2026-2028). It does not explicitly list the annual TAC figures for 2022, 2023, 2024, or 2025, although it notes the 2026 figure is a 17% increase over the prior period.

sources.title: ICCAT Recommendation 22-08: Multi-annual Management Plan for Bluefin Tuna in the Eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022–2026)

*   **2022:** This document (Rec. 22-08) amends Rec. 21-08 and establishes the management plan starting in 2023. The 2022 TAC is not explicitly listed in this document, but it confirms the transition from a recovery plan to a management plan.
*   **2023–2025:** The TAC for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna is set at **40,570 t** for each year (2023, 2024, and 2025), inclusive of dead discards. 
*   **2026:** The TAC for 2026 and thereafter is scheduled to be decided at the **2025 Commission Annual Meeting** in accordance with the Management Procedure (MP).

### Annual Quota Allocation by CPC (2023–2025)
The following annual quotas (in tonnes) apply for each year from 2023 to 2025:
*   **European Union:** 21,503 t
*   **Morocco:** 3,700 t
*   **Japan:** 3,114 t
*   **Tunisia:** 3,000 t
*   **Türkiye:** 2,600 t
*   **Libya:** 2,548 t
*   **Algeria:** 2,023 t
*   **Egypt:** 513 t
*   **Norway:** 368 t
*   **Albania:** 264 t
*   **Iceland:** 224 t
*   **Korea:** 221 t
*   **Syria:** 129 t
*   **China:** 112 t
*   **Chinese Taipei:** 101 t
*   **United Kingdom:** 63 t
*   **Namibia:** 50 t
*   **Unallocated Reserves:** 37 t
*   **Total:** 40,570 t

Note: Mauritania and Senegal may each catch up to 5 t for research annually, deducted from the unallocated reserve. Chinese Taipei may transfer up to 50 t of its quota to Korea in 2023–2025.

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results

*   **Transition to Management Plan:** The Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) advised moving from the previous Recovery Plan (Rec. 17-07) to a Management Plan because the current status of the stock no longer requires emergency measures.
*   **Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE):** The TACs for 2023–2025 were established based on a Management Procedure (MP) adopted at the 2022 Annual Meeting. The SCRS completed an MSE to establish this MP, which includes specific Harvest Control Rules (HCR).
*   **Recruitment and Sustainability:** The decision-making process considered the stock's capability to respond to consecutive years of low recruitment. It emphasized that fishing capacity must remain within sustainable limits and that control of capacity remains effective.
*   **SCRS Advice (Rec. 22-09):** The management plan is specifically based on the Management Procedure for Atlantic bluefin tuna used for both the western and eastern management areas, as defined in Recommendation 22-09.
*   **Historical Context:** The 2022 decision recognized the impacts of the previous Recovery Plan on small-scale fleets and the subsequent stabilization of the stock, allowing for the move to the MP-based TAC setting.

sources.title: ICCAT Recommendation 23-06: Amending the Multi-annual Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna
sources.content:
ICCAT Recommendation 23-06 (Amending Recommendation 22-08) is a technical update to the multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the Eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean. While the document does not explicitly list the specific Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas for each year from 2022 to 2026 within its text (as these were established in parent recommendations Rec. 21-08 and Rec. 22-08), it provides the following context and updated management measures:

- **Management Framework:** This recommendation amends Rec. 22-08, which itself updated Rec. 21-08. It addresses ambiguities and errors identified during the Intersessional Meeting of Panel 2 (March 2023) and the 16th Meeting of the Working Group on Integrated Monitoring Measures (IMM) (June 2023).
- **Farming Capacity and Input Quotas:** Each farm CPC must establish an annual farming management plan. The annual maximum input of wild-caught bluefin tuna into farms is restricted to the levels registered with ICCAT by each CPC's farms in 2005, 2006, 2007, or 2008. Any increase in maximum input must be commensurate with the specific fishing opportunities (quotas) allocated to that CPC, including live bluefin tuna imports.
- **Scientific and Monitoring Decisions:** The ICCAT Secretariat is tasked with compiling statistics on annual caging (input of wild fish), harvesting, and export using the electronic Bluefin Tuna Catch Document (eBCD) system. The Standing Catch Document Scheme Working Group (CDS WG) is responsible for developing data extraction functionality for these statistics.
- **Operational Procedures:** Donor operators must provide identical copies of video records of the first transfer and any voluntary/control transfers to the ICCAT regional observer and the receiving vessel. Regional Observer Program (ROP) providers must supply at least 25 ICCAT seals to each observer for use on purse seine vessels, traps, or towing vessels.
- **Exemptions:** Transfer provisions (Annex 10) do not apply to the release of bluefin tuna from traps resulting from lifting the gear at the end of the activity.

Note: The TAC for 2022 was set at 36,000 t by Rec. 21-08. The TAC for 2023, 2024, and 2025 was set at 40,570 t per year by Rec. 22-08. The scientific basis for the 2023-2025 quotas involved the 2022 SCRS stock assessment and the adoption of a Management Procedure (MP) as detailed in Rec. 22-09. This 23-06 document serves to refine the monitoring and farming aspects of that plan.

evaluation.url_relevance_rationale: Several URLs are strong primary ICCAT sources for exactly this query, including the 2022 stock assessment report, the 2024 executive summary, the 2024 SCRS report, and Recommendations 22-08 and 23-06. However, some URLs are tangential or wrong-stock/secondary sources, especially the NOAA advisory presentation, the Western Atlantic Recommendation 25-05, and the EU news item, so the set is not uniformly well targeted.
evaluation.tailored_comprehensiveness_rationale: The extracts do provide concrete TAC figures for 2022 and 2023-2025, plus scientific details from the 2022 assessment such as model platforms and F/SSB findings. But coverage for 2026 is fragmented and relies on a press release and EU news rather than a formal recommendation or assessment extract, and several extracts do not actually contain the year-by-year scientific basis behind each decision in a clean, direct way.
evaluation.synthesis_quality_rationale: The synthesis mostly stays within the extracted content and correctly reports 36,000 t for 2022, 40,570 t for 2023-2025, and 48,403 t for 2026. Still, it overstates support in places: the claim that the 2022 TAC 'marked the final phase of the transition' is more interpretive than directly extracted, and the statement that the 2025 decision used 'updated scientific indicators processed through the MP' is only loosely supported by the provided extracts rather than explicitly documented in a formal ICCAT recommendation here.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
Here is the year-by-year ICCAT **Total Allowable Catch (TAC)** for **Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (BFT-E)** from **2022 through 2026**, together with the **scientific basis ICCAT cited** and the **stock assessment / status results behind each decision**.

## Short answer: TACs by year

- **2022:** **36,000 t**. ICCAT Rec. 21-08 states that the TAC for 2022 “shall be set at 36,000 t,” explicitly “in accordance with the SCRS advice.” [RECOMMENDATION BY ICCAT AMENDING THE RECOMMENDATION 19-04 AMENDING RECOMMENDATION 18-02 ESTABLISHING A MULTI-ANNUAL MANAGEMENT PLAN FOR BLUEFIN TUNA IN THE EASTERN ATLANTIC AND THE MEDITERRANEAN](https://www.iccat.int/com2022/ENG/PLE_102_ENG.pdf)
- **2023:** **40,570 t**. ICCAT Rec. 22-08 set the TAC for **2023–2025** at **40,570 t** “in accordance with the MP” (Management Procedure). [22-08 BFT RECOMMENDATION BY ICCAT AMENDING THE R](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)
- **2024:** **40,570 t**. The same Rec. 22-08 kept the TAC at **40,570 t** for 2024 as part of the fixed 2023–2025 block. [22-08 BFT RECOMMENDATION BY ICCAT AMENDING THE R](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)
- **2025:** **40,570 t**. The same Rec. 22-08 kept the TAC at **40,570 t** for 2025. The 2024/2025 SCRS executive summary says the TAC for 2023–2025 is 40,570 t and that under the Exceptional Circumstances protocol no deviation was warranted for 2025. [22-08 BFT RECOMMENDATION BY ICCAT AMENDING THE R](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT REPORT 2024-2025 (I)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf)
- **2026:** I did **not find evidence in the sources retrieved that ICCAT has yet adopted a new numeric TAC for 2026** for BFT-E.
